<!doctype html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
<meta charset="utf-8" />
<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width,initial-scale=1" />
<title>Heyting algebras and toposes</title>
<link rel="stylesheet" href="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/katex@0.16.11/dist/katex.min.css">
<script defer src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/katex@0.16.11/dist/katex.min.js"></script>
<script defer src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/katex@0.16.11/dist/contrib/auto-render.min.js"></script>
<script>
(function(){
  function renderAll(){
    if(typeof renderMathInElement!=='function'){ return setTimeout(renderAll,50); }
    renderMathInElement(document.body,{
      delimiters:[
        {left:'$$',right:'$$',display:true},
        {left:'$',right:'$',display:false},
        {left:'\\(',right:'\\)',display:false},
        {left:'\\[',right:'\\]',display:true}
      ],
      throwOnError:false,
      macros:{
        '\\Spec':'\\Spec',
        '\\Gal':'\\Gal',
        '\\Hom':'\\Hom',
        '\\tr':'\\tr',
        '\\ad':'\\ad',
        '\\ind':'\\ind'
      }
    });
  }
  if(document.readyState==='loading') document.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded',renderAll);
  else renderAll();
})();
</script>
<style>
  :root{
    --bg:#0f1218; --panel:#161b24; --panel2:#1c2230;
    --ink:#e8ecf1; --mute:#9aa4b2; --line:#2a3242;
    --yellow:#ffd866; --blue:#58c4dd; --green:#83c167;
    --pink:#e07a5f; --violet:#b39ddb; --cyan:#7de0d6;
  }
  *{box-sizing:border-box}
  html,body{margin:0;padding:0;background:var(--bg);color:var(--ink);
    font-family:-apple-system,BlinkMacSystemFont,"Segoe UI",Inter,Roboto,sans-serif;
    font-size:17px;line-height:1.65}
  a{color:var(--blue)}
  code{font-family:ui-monospace,SFMono-Regular,Menlo,Consolas,monospace;
    background:#0b0f16;padding:1px 6px;border-radius:4px;color:#f0c987;font-size:0.92em}
  main{max-width:880px;margin:0 auto;padding:4rem 1.5rem 8rem}
  h1{font-size:2.4rem;letter-spacing:-0.01em;margin:0 0 0.4rem}
  h2{font-size:1.6rem;margin:3.2rem 0 0.8rem;color:#fff;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);padding-bottom:.4rem}
  h3{font-size:1.15rem;margin:1.8rem 0 .6rem;color:var(--yellow)}
  p{margin:0.7rem 0}
  .sub{color:var(--mute);margin-top:0;margin-bottom:2rem}
  .widget{background:var(--panel);border:1px solid var(--line);border-radius:10px;
    padding:1rem 1.1rem 1.2rem;margin:1.2rem 0}
  .widget .hd{display:flex;justify-content:space-between;align-items:baseline;margin-bottom:.6rem;gap:.8rem;flex-wrap:wrap}
  .widget .hd .ttl{font-weight:600;color:#fff;font-size:.95rem;letter-spacing:.02em;text-transform:uppercase}
  .widget .hd .hint{color:var(--mute);font-size:.85rem}
  .row{display:flex;gap:.6rem;flex-wrap:wrap;align-items:center;margin:.4rem 0}
  .row label{color:var(--mute);font-size:.88rem}
  input[type="text"],input[type="number"],select{
    background:#0b0f16;color:var(--ink);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:6px;padding:.35rem .55rem;font-family:inherit;font-size:.95rem}
  input[type="range"]{accent-color:var(--yellow);flex:1;max-width:260px}
  button{background:var(--panel2);color:var(--ink);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:6px;padding:.35rem .7rem;cursor:pointer;font:inherit}
  button:hover{background:#242c3e}
  button.active{background:var(--yellow);color:#111;border-color:var(--yellow)}
  .readout{background:#0b0f16;border:1px solid var(--line);border-radius:6px;
    padding:.6rem .8rem;font-family:ui-monospace,monospace;font-size:.92rem;color:#cbd5e1;
    min-height:1.6em;white-space:pre-wrap;word-break:break-word}
  .pill{display:inline-block;background:var(--panel2);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:999px;padding:1px 8px;font-size:.82rem;color:var(--mute);margin-right:4px}
  .note{border-left:3px solid var(--violet);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#d8d2ef;background:#1a1828;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  .ok{border-left:3px solid var(--green);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#d5ebc8;background:#1a2418;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  .bad{border-left:3px solid var(--pink);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#f4d2c7;background:#2a1a18;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  svg{display:block;margin:0 auto;max-width:100%}
  svg text{fill:var(--ink);font-family:inherit}
  nav.toc{position:sticky;top:0;background:rgba(15,18,24,.93);backdrop-filter:blur(8px);
    border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);padding:.6rem 1rem;z-index:10;
    display:flex;gap:1rem;flex-wrap:wrap;font-size:.9rem}
  nav.toc a{color:var(--mute);text-decoration:none}
  nav.toc a:hover{color:var(--yellow)}
  /* sticky sidebar TOC (wide screens only; narrow screens fall back to top-nav) */
  aside.sidetoc{position:fixed;top:4.2rem;
    left:max(1rem, calc(50% - 440px - 220px));
    width:200px;max-height:calc(100vh - 6rem);overflow-y:auto;
    padding-right:1rem;border-right:1px solid var(--line);
    font-size:.88rem;z-index:5}
  aside.sidetoc .sidetoc-title{font-size:.7rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute);margin:0 0 .5rem;padding-bottom:.35rem;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line)}
  aside.sidetoc ol{list-style:none;padding:0;margin:0}
  aside.sidetoc li{margin:0}
  aside.sidetoc a{display:block;padding:.3rem .5rem;color:var(--mute);text-decoration:none;
    border-left:2px solid transparent;line-height:1.35;
    transition:color 120ms ease, border-color 120ms ease}
  aside.sidetoc a:hover{color:var(--ink)}
  aside.sidetoc a.active{color:var(--yellow);border-left-color:var(--yellow)}
  @media (max-width:1180px){ aside.sidetoc{display:none} }
  @media (min-width:1181px){ nav.toc a[href^="#"]{display:none} }
  .hero{padding:3rem 0 1rem}
  .small{font-size:.88rem;color:var(--mute)}
  footer{color:var(--mute);font-size:.88rem;text-align:center;margin-top:4rem}
  table.plain{width:100%;border-collapse:collapse;font-size:.95rem;margin:.6rem 0}
  table.plain th,table.plain td{padding:.35rem .5rem;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);text-align:left}
  table.plain th{color:var(--mute);font-weight:500}
  /* callback-css-auto-begin */
  aside.callback{
    margin:1.2rem 0;padding:.7rem 1rem;
    background:rgba(88,196,221,0.05);
    border-left:3px solid color-mix(in srgb, var(--cyan) 45%, transparent);
    border-radius:0 6px 6px 0;
    font-size:.93rem;
  }
  aside.callback .ttl{
    font-size:.68rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);margin-bottom:.3rem;font-weight:600;
  }
  aside.callback ul{margin:0;padding-left:1.1rem}
  aside.callback ul li{margin:.15rem 0}
  aside.callback a{color:inherit}
/* callback-css-auto-end */
  details.changelog{
    margin:2rem 0 1rem;padding:.6rem .9rem;
    background:rgba(0,0,0,0.22);
    border-radius:6px;
    font-size:.85rem;
  }
  details.changelog summary{
    cursor:pointer;letter-spacing:.1em;text-transform:uppercase;
    font-size:.7rem;color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);
  }
  details.changelog table{margin-top:.5rem;border-collapse:collapse;width:100%;font-size:.82rem}
  details.changelog td{padding:.2rem .6rem;border-bottom:1px dashed rgba(255,255,255,0.07);vertical-align:top}
  details.changelog td:first-child{color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);white-space:nowrap;font-variant-numeric:tabular-nums}
  aside.related{
    margin:1.2rem 0;padding:.7rem 1rem;
    background:color-mix(in srgb, var(--mute) 6%, transparent);
    border-left:3px solid color-mix(in srgb, var(--mute) 55%, transparent);
    border-radius:0 6px 6px 0;
    font-size:.93rem;
  }
  aside.related .ttl{
    font-size:.68rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);margin-bottom:.3rem;font-weight:600;
  }
  aside.related > div{margin:.15rem 0}
  aside.related .more{color:var(--mute);font-style:italic}
  aside.related a{color:inherit}
  /* display-prefs-css-auto-begin */
  html[data-hide-widgets] .widget { display: none !important; }
  html[data-hide-quizzes] .quiz { display: none !important; }
  html[data-hide-lineage] .lineage-strip { display: none !important; }
  .mv-display-toggles { display: inline-flex; gap: .6rem; align-items: center; }
  .mv-display-toggle { background: transparent; border: 1px solid var(--line, #333); border-radius: 6px; padding: 5px 10px; cursor: pointer; font-size: 1.05rem; line-height: 1; color: inherit; transition: background 120ms ease, border-color 120ms ease; }
  .mv-display-toggle:hover { background: var(--panel, rgba(255,255,255,0.06)); border-color: var(--mute, #666); }
  .mv-display-toggle--off { opacity: 0.35; }
  /* display-prefs-css-auto-end */
</style>

Heyting algebras and toposes

Intuitionist logic inside any topos: $\Omega$ as a Heyting algebra and the Mitchell–Bénabou language.

blocks.html:
1. Heyting algebras

A Heyting algebra is a bounded lattice $H$ equipped with a binary operation $\Rightarrow$, the relative pseudo-complement or implication, satisfying the adjunction $$a \wedge b \;\le\; c \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad a \;\le\; b \Rightarrow c.$$ Equivalently, $b \Rightarrow c$ is the largest element of $H$ whose meet with $b$ lands below $c$. From this the negation $ eg a := a \Rightarrow ot$ falls out automatically, and one proves the intuitionist tautologies $a \le eg eg a$, $ eg eg eg a = eg a$, and the Heyting–De Morgan law $ eg(a \vee b) = eg a \wedge eg b$. The "missing" classical equation $ eg eg a = a$ is exactly what fails in general — it is equivalent to the law of excluded middle $a \vee eg a = op$.

Two streams feed the definition. Algebraic side: a Heyting algebra is to intuitionist propositional logic what a Boolean algebra is to classical propositional logic — its filters are the consistent theories, its primes encode propositional models, and its Lindenbaum algebra is the free Heyting algebra on the propositional letters. Geometric side: the open sets of any topological space form a Heyting algebra under $\cap, \cup$, with $U \Rightarrow V := \mathrm{int}(U^c \cup V)$ and $ eg U = \mathrm{int}(U^c)$. This is the prototype: open-set lattices are essentially the only Heyting algebras one ever needs (Stone-style duality theorems make this precise via locales).

Boolean algebras are the special case where $ eg eg a = a$ for all $a$. Equivalently, a Heyting algebra is Boolean iff it is complemented, iff every element is regular ($ eg eg a = a$), iff LEM holds. The 4-element diamond $\{ ot, a, b, op\}$ below is the smallest Boolean algebra besides $\{ ot, op\}$; the open-set lattice on a connected non-Hausdorff space (e.g. the Sierpiński space) is the smallest non-Boolean Heyting algebra. Click any pair of elements to compute $a \wedge b$, $a \vee b$, $a \Rightarrow b$, and $ eg a$ from the order alone.

2. $\Omega$ as an internal Heyting algebra

The subobject classifier $\Omega$ in a topos $\mathcal{E}$ does more than name truth values — it carries internal lattice operations. The trick is universality: a map $\Omega \times \Omega \to \Omega$ is, by classification, a subobject of $\Omega \times \Omega$. So defining $\wedge, \vee, \Rightarrow$ as morphisms is the same as picking out the right subobjects of $\Omega \times \Omega$, then taking their characteristic maps.

Concretely: $\wedge\colon \Omega\times\Omega \to \Omega$ classifies the diagonal subobject $\langle\mathrm{true},\mathrm{true}\rangle\colon 1 \hookrightarrow \Omega \times \Omega$ — the unique pair both equal to $\top$. $\vee$ classifies the image of the map $1 + 1 \to \Omega \times \Omega$ that sends $(*, *)$ to $(\top, x)$ on one summand and $(x, \top)$ on the other (factoring through the union of the two coordinate-true lines). $\Rightarrow$ classifies $\{(p,q) \in \Omega\times\Omega : p \le q\}$ — the partial-order relation on $\Omega$ itself, which is in turn the equaliser of $\wedge\colon \Omega\times\Omega \to \Omega$ and the first projection $\pi_1$. Negation is the special case $\neg = \big(\Omega \xrightarrow{\langle 1, !\circ\bot\rangle} \Omega\times\Omega \xrightarrow{\Rightarrow} \Omega\big)$ — apply implication against $\bot$.

That this internal structure is a Heyting algebra (and not, generically, a Boolean one) is forced by the way subobjects glue: in $\mathbf{Set}$ the lattice is $\{\bot,\top\}$ and is Boolean, but in $G$-$\mathbf{Set}$ the elements of $\Omega$ are the $G$-stable subgroups of $G$ (the subgroup lattice ordered by inclusion), in $\widehat{C}$ the elements over $c$ are sieves on $c$, and in $\mathrm{Sh}(X)$ the elements over $U$ are open subsets of $U$. None of these are Boolean in general. Externally, $\mathrm{Sub}(X)$ inherits the operations componentwise: $S \wedge T = S \cap T$, $S \vee T = S \cup T$, and $S \Rightarrow T$ is the largest subobject $W \subseteq X$ with $W \cap S \subseteq T$.

The scrubber below walks the construction step by step: subobject of $\Omega\times\Omega$, classifying map, lattice axiom check.

Heyting algebras and toposes · The internal Mitchell–Bénabou language

3. The internal Mitchell–Bénabou language

The Mitchell–Bénabou language of a topos $\mathcal{E}$ is a typed higher-order intuitionist language whose syntax is interpreted directly by morphisms of $\mathcal{E}$. The dictionary is mechanical: every type $A$ names an object $\llbracket A \rrbracket$ of $\mathcal{E}$ (with $1$ for the unit type and $\Omega$ for the propositional type), every term $t \colon A$-in-context-$\Gamma$ names a morphism $\llbracket \Gamma \rrbracket \to \llbracket A \rrbracket$, and every formula $\varphi$ — a term of type $\Omega$ — names a characteristic map $\llbracket \Gamma \rrbracket \to \Omega$, equivalently a subobject of $\llbracket \Gamma \rrbracket$.

Logical operators are interpreted via the internal Heyting structure built in §2. Conjunction $\varphi \wedge \psi$ becomes $\Omega\times\Omega \xrightarrow{\wedge} \Omega$ pre-composed with $\langle\llbracket\varphi\rrbracket, \llbracket\psi\rrbracket\rangle$; quantifiers come from adjoints to pullback along projection: $\exists$ is the left adjoint $\Sigma_\pi$ (image), $\forall$ is the right adjoint $\Pi_\pi$ (the largest subobject whose preimage lies in the given one). Equality $t =_A s$ classifies the diagonal $\Delta_A\colon A \hookrightarrow A \times A$ pulled back along $\langle t, s \rangle$. The constant $\mathrm{true}$ is the top of $\mathrm{Sub}$; provability in the natural-deduction calculus matches subobject inclusion, $\Gamma \vdash \varphi$ iff $\llbracket\top_\Gamma\rrbracket \le \llbracket\varphi\rrbracket$ in $\mathrm{Sub}(\llbracket\Gamma\rrbracket)$.

Two consequences. First, every theorem of intuitionist higher-order logic is true in every topos — the Mitchell–Bénabou language is a conservative reading of $\mathcal{E}$. Second, classical principles (LEM, choice, decidable equality) translate to specific topos-theoretic conditions on $\mathcal{E}$: LEM corresponds to "every subobject has a complement," AC to "every epi splits," etc. So a topos sees its own classical-vs-intuitionist character in this language. Mac Lane–Moerdijk VI.5–7 develops the full apparatus; Goldblatt's Topoi §11 is the textbook entry point.

4. Kripke–Joyal forcing semantics

The internal-language interpretation of §3 is global: a formula either holds (its subobject is everything) or it doesn't. Kripke–Joyal semantics refines this by tracking which generalised elements $\alpha\colon X \to \llbracket\Gamma\rrbracket$ make $\varphi$ true. Write $X \Vdash_\alpha \varphi$ — read "$X$ forces $\varphi$ at $\alpha$" — for the relation $\alpha$ factors through $\{\Gamma \mid \varphi\}$. The whole intuitionist calculus reduces to bookkeeping about generalised elements: $$\begin{aligned} X &\Vdash_\alpha \varphi\wedge\psi &&\iff X \Vdash_\alpha \varphi \;\text{and}\; X\Vdash_\alpha\psi,\\ X &\Vdash_\alpha \varphi\vee\psi &&\iff \exists\;\text{cover}\; X = \bigsqcup X_i,\;\text{each}\; X_i \Vdash \varphi \;\text{or}\; X_i\Vdash\psi,\\ X &\Vdash_\alpha \varphi\to\psi &&\iff \forall f\colon Y\to X,\; Y\Vdash_{f^*\alpha}\varphi \implies Y\Vdash_{f^*\alpha}\psi,\\ X &\Vdash_\alpha \exists y.\varphi &&\iff \exists\;\text{cover}\; \{f_i\colon X_i \to X\}\;\text{and}\; b_i,\; X_i \Vdash \varphi(b_i),\\ X &\Vdash_\alpha \forall y.\varphi &&\iff \forall f\colon Y\to X, b\colon Y\to A,\; Y\Vdash \varphi(b). \end{aligned}$$ Notice the asymmetry: $\to$ and $\forall$ quantify over all $f\colon Y\to X$ (a "stability" or persistence condition), while $\vee$ and $\exists$ require some cover. That asymmetry is exactly what makes the logic intuitionist.

In a presheaf topos $\widehat{C}$ this rule set specialises to the classical Kripke possible-worlds semantics. Worlds are objects of $C$, the accessibility relation is "there is an arrow $c \to c'$", and the persistence clauses for $\to,\forall$ become "if $c$ forces $\varphi\to\psi$ and $c \to c'$, then $c'$ forces $\varphi\to\psi$" — the classic monotonicity built into Kripke models. In a sheaf topos $\mathrm{Sh}(X)$, $X \Vdash \varphi$ means the formula is true on a covering family of opens; this is the topos-theoretic source of constructive analysis ("$\varphi$ holds locally").

The scrubber below walks Kripke–Joyal forcing through a 2-world Kripke model $C$: object $a \to b$ generated by a single arrow. Watch how $\neg p \vee p$ fails at $a$ but holds at $b$.

See also
  • Presheaf toposes $\hat{C} = [C^{\mathrm{op}}, \mathbf{Set}]$

Used in
Heyting algebras and toposes · Why LEM fails: double negation in toposes

5. Why LEM fails: double negation in toposes

The fundamental fact is that $\Omega$ is generally not a Boolean algebra, so the law of excluded middle $\varphi \vee eg\varphi = op$ is not a topos-theoretic theorem. The cleanest single example is the open-set lattice of $\mathbb{R}$: take $U = (-\infty,0)$ as a "proposition." Then $ eg U = \mathrm{int}(U^c) = (0,\infty)$, and $U \vee eg U = (-\infty,0) \cup (0,\infty)$ misses the point $0$. So $U \vee eg U e \mathbb{R}$ — LEM fails. Worse, $ eg eg U = \mathrm{int}(\overline{U}) = (-\infty,0]^\circ = (-\infty,0) = U$ in this case, but for $V = \mathbb{R} \setminus \{0\}$ we get $ eg eg V = \mathbb{R} e V$. Double-negation is a non-trivial closure operator.

The systematic fix is the double-negation topology $ eg eg$ on any topos $\mathcal{E}$. It is a Lawvere–Tierney topology — an internal closure operator $j\colon \Omega o \Omega$ — and the category $\mathrm{Sh}_{ eg eg}(\mathcal{E})$ of $j$-sheaves is the largest Boolean subtopos of $\mathcal{E}$ (Mac Lane–Moerdijk V.2). Inside $\mathrm{Sh}_{ eg eg}$, LEM is restored: every $ eg eg$-stable proposition is decidable. This is the topos-theoretic shadow of Cohen forcing — for the right base topos (sets of names) the $ eg eg$-sheaves model ZFC where the Continuum Hypothesis fails.

A topos $\mathcal{E}$ is Boolean when $\Omega$ is internally a Boolean algebra, equivalently when every subobject has a complement. The two canonical Boolean toposes are $\mathbf{Set}$ itself and $G$-$\mathbf{Set}$ (sets with a $G$-action) for $G$ any group. Presheaf toposes $\widehat{C}$ for $C$ not a groupoid are non-Boolean; sheaves on a non-discrete topological space are non-Boolean. Browse a small case library below — pick a topos and read off whether $ eg eg p = p$ and LEM hold.

blocks.html:
6. Geometric morphisms preserve geometric logic

A geometric morphism $f\colon \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{E}$ is an adjoint pair $f^* \dashv f_*$ in which the inverse image $f^*\colon \mathcal{E} \to \mathcal{F}$ preserves finite limits (and arbitrary colimits, since it is a left adjoint). This is precisely the structure that pulls back $\Omega_\mathcal{E}$ to a Heyting algebra in $\mathcal{F}$ and lifts subobjects across the morphism. The constraint that $f^*$ preserves finite limits — not just arbitrary ones — is the categorical content of "$f$ is logical for the geometric fragment."

Geometric logic is the fragment of intuitionist first-order logic generated by $\top, \bot, \wedge$ (binary), $\bigvee$ (arbitrary, including infinitary disjunction), $\exists$, and equality. What is omitted: $\Rightarrow, \neg, \forall$, and infinitary conjunction. The exact match is no accident: $f^*$ preserves $\wedge$ because it preserves finite products of subobjects (i.e. pullbacks). It preserves $\bigvee$ because arbitrary colimits — in particular the union of a family of subobjects — are images of coproducts, and $f^*$ commutes with both. It preserves $\exists$ because images go to images (left adjoints commute with images). It preserves equality because it preserves diagonals. Conversely $f^*$ does not in general preserve $\Rightarrow$ or $\forall$: those are constructed via right adjoints to pullback, and a left adjoint need not commute with right adjoints.

The big consequence is geometric soundness: a geometric sequent $\varphi \vdash_x \psi$ valid in $\mathcal{E}$ is automatically valid in $\mathcal{F}$ for any geometric morphism $\mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{E}$. This is the engine behind classifying-topos arguments: if $\mathcal{T}$ is a geometric theory, the topos $\mathrm{Set}[\mathcal{T}]$ classifies $\mathcal{T}$-models in any topos via "geometric morphisms $\mathcal{F} \to \mathrm{Set}[\mathcal{T}]$ correspond to $\mathcal{T}$-models in $\mathcal{F}$." Composition $g\circ f$ has $(g\circ f)^* = f^* \circ g^*$, so geometric formulas survive arbitrary chains of geometric morphisms — pull a geometric truth all the way back along Spec$(R) \to$ Spec$(\mathbb{Z}) \to *$ and it stays geometric. Click the connectives below to see which transport.

blocks.html:
See also
  • Geometric morphisms

7. Connections

Heyting algebras and the internal logic they organise are not a destination — they are a hinge between several theories that the rest of this notebook develops.

The internal logic and category theory

Every reasoning step you took inside $\mathcal{E}$ — the typing-tree judgments, the universally-quantified Kripke–Joyal clauses — is interpretable in any cartesian-closed category with a subobject classifier. The full account starts from cartesian-closed categories and power-object axioms; what we have here is the slice of that story specific to propositional reasoning.

Sheaf semantics and Grothendieck topoi

The Kripke–Joyal forcing relation $U \Vdash \varphi$ is the same as local-truth in a sheaf: a Grothendieck topology determines which covering families count, and forcing on the resulting topos becomes site-aware. Sites and sheaves is the next step if you want to build topoi rather than reason inside a fixed one.

Higher topoi and homotopy type theory

Replace sets with $\infty$-groupoids and the same internal-language game produces the language of $\infty$-topoi. The propositions live a layer below the higher truth-values, and the $\Omega$ of this page becomes a $(-1)$-truncated object. Univalence in homotopy type theory turns isomorphism into identity at every height.

Constructive mathematics

The double-negation translation in $\S 5$ embeds classical logic into intuitionistic logic — a precise way to say "classical mathematics is intuitionistic mathematics about double-negation-stable propositions". This is the logician's translation; first-order logic develops the syntactic side, and reverse mathematics studies which classical theorems require which non-constructive principles.

Open frontiers: realizability topoi (effectivity carriers a different internal logic), modal type theory (geometric morphisms become modal operators), and synthetic mathematics done inside a topos (synthetic differential geometry, synthetic stability theory) all exploit the same internal-language machinery.
